1.title: George Archer born in San Francisco
1.text: George Archer, the 1969 Masters champion and one of the finest putters of his era with 13 PGA Tour titles, was born on this day in San Francisco, California.

1.title: Open champion Jack Burns born
1.text: Scottish professional Jack Burns, winner of the 1888 Open Championship at St Andrews, was born around this time in Fife. He later returned to work as a railway platelayer.

1.title: Mark Brooks born in Fort Worth
1.text: Mark Brooks, winner of the 1996 PGA Championship in a playoff over Kenny Perry and seven PGA Tour titles in all, was born on this day in Fort Worth, Texas.

1.title: Grayson Murray born
1.text: Grayson Murray, a two-time PGA Tour winner whose victories included the 2017 Barbasol Championship and the 2024 Sony Open, was born on this day in Raleigh, North Carolina.

1.title: Tiger Woods wins American Express Championship
1.text: Tiger Woods closed out a dominant stretch by winning the WGC-American Express Championship at The Grove in England by eight strokes, his sixth consecutive PGA Tour victory.

1.title: Europe regains the Ryder Cup in Rome
1.text: Europe defeated the United States 16.5-11.5 at Marco Simone outside Rome to reclaim the Ryder Cup, with Tommy Fleetwood clinching the winning point.

2.title: John Cook born in Toledo
2.text: John Cook, an 11-time PGA Tour winner and 1993 U.S. Ryder Cup team member, was born on this day in Toledo, Ohio.

2.title: USA wins Ryder Cup at Hazeltine
2.text: The United States defeated Europe 17-11 at Hazeltine National in Minnesota to reclaim the Ryder Cup, the Americans' first win since 2008. Ryan Moore clinched the decisive point.

2.title: Mackenzie Hughes wins Sanderson Farms
2.text: Mackenzie Hughes of Canada beat Sepp Straka on the second playoff hole to win the Sanderson Farms Championship at the Country Club of Jackson, Mississippi.

2.title: Susie Maxwell Berning dies at 83
2.text: Susie Maxwell Berning, a three-time U.S. Women's Open champion (1968, 1972, 1973) and World Golf Hall of Fame member, died on this day at age 83.

2.title: Webb Simpson's breakout season
2.text: Webb Simpson capped a breakout 2011 campaign with multiple PGA Tour wins, establishing himself as a Ryder Cup contender and future U.S. Open champion.

3.title: Fred Couples born in Seattle
3.text: Fred Couples, the 1992 Masters champion and former World No. 1 known for his smooth swing and easygoing charisma, was born on this day in Seattle, Washington.

3.title: Harold Henning born in Johannesburg
3.text: Harold Henning, a South African with roughly 50 wins worldwide and a Ryder Cup-era international competitor, was born on this day in Johannesburg.

3.title: Sam Burns wins Sanderson Farms
3.text: Sam Burns won the Sanderson Farms Championship at the Country Club of Jackson, Mississippi, finishing 22-under to win by one stroke.

3.title: Europe wins Celtic Manor Ryder Cup on Monday
3.text: On a rain-delayed Monday finish, Europe beat the United States 14.5-13.5 at Celtic Manor in Wales, with Graeme McDowell securing the decisive point against Hunter Mahan.

3.title: Fred Couples's career year
3.text: In 1992 Fred Couples won the Masters and reached World No. 1, a peak season for the Seattle native who remained beloved by fans throughout his career.

4.title: Horace Rawlins wins the first U.S. Open
4.text: Englishman Horace Rawlins won the inaugural U.S. Open at Newport Golf Club in Rhode Island, shooting 173 over 36 holes in a single day to claim the first national championship.

4.title: Tom Kidd wins the first Open at St Andrews
4.text: Tom Kidd won the Open Championship at the Old Course, the first Open held away from Prestwick and the first whose champion received the Claret Jug.

4.title: Europe wins Ryder Cup at Celtic Manor
4.text: Europe's 14.5-13.5 victory at Celtic Manor in Wales, completed on a rain-forced Monday, was the first Ryder Cup ever to finish on a Monday.

4.title: Emiliano Grillo wins Frys.com Open
4.text: Emiliano Grillo of Argentina beat Kevin Na in a playoff to win the Frys.com Open in California for his first PGA Tour title, capping a strong rookie campaign.

4.title: The Claret Jug era begins
4.text: With Tom Kidd's 1873 win at St Andrews, the Open Championship introduced the Claret Jug, which remains golf's most iconic trophy.

5.title: Laura Davies born in England
5.text: Laura Davies, a World Golf Hall of Fame member with four major titles and 20 LPGA Tour wins, was born on this day in Coventry, England.

5.title: U.S. Amateur champion Kelly Kraft born
5.text: Kelly Kraft, who won the 2011 U.S. Amateur with a 2-up victory over Patrick Cantlay, was born on this day in Denton, Texas.

5.title: Ben Martin wins Shriners Open
5.text: Ben Martin won the Shriners Hospitals for Children Open at TPC Summerlin in Las Vegas for his first PGA Tour title.

5.title: The Great Triumvirate era
5.text: By the early 1900s Harry Vardon, J.H. Taylor, and James Braid dominated the Open Championship, a rivalry that defined British golf for two decades.

5.title: Curtis Strange tops the money list
5.text: Curtis Strange led the PGA Tour money list in 1985, a peak season for the player who would win back-to-back U.S. Opens in 1988 and 1989.

6.title: Walter Hagen dies at 76
6.text: Walter Hagen, the flamboyant 11-time major champion who transformed the status of the touring professional, died on this day at age 76.

6.title: Bob May born
6.text: Bob May, who famously pushed Tiger Woods to a playoff at the 2000 PGA Championship at Valhalla in one of the great major duels, was born on this day.

6.title: Kevin Na wins Shriners in playoff
6.text: Kevin Na beat Patrick Cantlay in a playoff to win the Shriners Hospitals for Children Open at TPC Summerlin in Las Vegas.

6.title: Kevin Yu wins Sanderson Farms
6.text: Kevin Yu beat Beau Hossler on the first playoff hole to win the Sanderson Farms Championship in Mississippi for his first PGA Tour title.

6.title: Walter Hagen's showman era
6.text: By the early 1920s Walter Hagen was the game's first great professional showman, a figure who would win five PGA Championships and transform the pro's standing.

7.title: Ryder Cup player Peter Baker born
7.text: Englishman Peter Baker, a member of Europe's victorious 1993 Ryder Cup team at The Belfry, was born on this day.

7.title: Kevin Tway wins Safeway Open
7.text: Kevin Tway beat Ryan Moore in a playoff to win the Safeway Open at Silverado in Napa, California, for his first PGA Tour title.

7.title: Steve Stricker's comeback year
7.text: Steve Stricker capped a remarkable career resurgence in 2007, named PGA Tour Comeback Player of the Year as he climbed back into the world's top ranks.

7.title: Autumn golf tradition in Britain
7.text: In the early 1900s the Open Championship and other British events were often contested in early autumn before the modern summer schedule was fixed.

7.title: Peter Baker's Ryder Cup heroics
7.text: Peter Baker was one of Europe's standout rookies at the 1993 Ryder Cup, contributing crucial points in a narrow defeat at The Belfry.

8.title: Brendan Steele defends Safeway Open
8.text: Brendan Steele successfully defended his title at the Safeway Open at Silverado in Napa, California, winning by two over Tony Finau.

8.title: Luke List wins Sanderson Farms in playoff
8.text: Luke List won a five-man playoff at the Sanderson Farms Championship in Mississippi, holing a 45-foot birdie putt to claim the title.

8.title: Pre-war professional era
8.text: The years between the world wars saw professional golf expand rapidly in both Britain and America, building the foundations of the modern tours.

8.title: Curtis Strange's back-to-back U.S. Opens
8.text: Curtis Strange's repeat U.S. Open title in 1989 made him the first to defend the championship since Ben Hogan in 1951, a defining achievement of the era.

8.title: Tony Finau's rising-star autumn
8.text: Tony Finau's runner-up finish at the 2017 Safeway Open was part of a steady climb that established him among the PGA Tour's most consistent contenders.

9.title: Annika Sorenstam born in Sweden
9.text: Annika Sorenstam, the most dominant women's golfer of her era with 72 LPGA Tour wins and 10 major championships, was born on this day in Sweden.

9.title: Tiger Woods wins American Express at Harding Park
9.text: Tiger Woods defeated John Daly on the second playoff hole to win the WGC-American Express Championship at Harding Park in San Francisco, his tenth World Golf Championship title.

9.title: Tom Kim wins Shriners Children's Open
9.text: South Korea's Tom Kim won the Shriners Children's Open at TPC Summerlin in Las Vegas for his second PGA Tour title at age 20.

9.title: John Daly's near-miss at Harding Park
9.text: John Daly came within a short putt of beating Tiger Woods in the 2005 WGC-American Express playoff at Harding Park, missing from close range on the second extra hole.

9.title: Annika Sorenstam's dominant prime
9.text: Around her birthday in the early 2000s, Annika Sorenstam was rewriting the LPGA record books, including a famous appearance against the men at the 2003 Colonial.

10.title: Bruce Devlin born in Australia
10.text: Bruce Devlin, an Australian who won eight PGA Tour titles and became a noted broadcaster and course architect, was born on this day.

10.title: Sungjae Im wins Shriners Children's Open
10.text: Sungjae Im closed with a 62 to win the Shriners Children's Open at TPC Summerlin by four strokes, finishing at a tournament-record 24-under par.

10.title: Tiger Woods wins Disney Classic
10.text: Tiger Woods won the National Car Rental Golf Classic at Walt Disney World in Florida, part of a dominant late-season stretch in 1999.

10.title: Ben Crane wins Frys.com Open
10.text: Ben Crane won the Frys.com Open in California for one of his PGA Tour titles during the early fall events.

10.title: Sungjae Im ties tournament scoring record
10.text: Sungjae Im's 24-under total at the 2021 Shriners Children's Open matched the lowest winning score in the event's history at TPC Summerlin.

11.title: Michelle Wie West born in Honolulu
11.text: Michelle Wie West, a child prodigy who won the 2014 U.S. Women's Open and helped popularize the women's game worldwide, was born on this day in Honolulu, Hawaii.

11.title: Tom Vardon born in Jersey
11.text: Tom Vardon, a course architect and brother of six-time Open champion Harry Vardon, was born around this time on the island of Jersey. He finished runner-up at the 1903 Open.

11.title: Greg Chalmers born in Sydney
11.text: Greg Chalmers, a two-time Australian Open champion who won the 2016 Barracuda Championship for his first PGA Tour title, was born on this day in Sydney.

11.title: Nick Faldo's swing rebuild
11.text: In the mid-1980s Nick Faldo undertook a famous rebuild of his swing under coach David Leadbetter, a project that would lead to six major championships.

11.title: Martin Laird wins Shriners in playoff
11.text: Scotland's Martin Laird won the Shriners Hospitals for Children Open at TPC Summerlin in a playoff, ending a long victory drought on the PGA Tour.

12.title: Cristie Kerr born in Miami
12.text: Cristie Kerr, a 20-time LPGA Tour winner, two-time major champion, and former World No. 1, was born on this day in Miami, Florida.

12.title: Sally Little born in South Africa
12.text: Sally Little, a 15-time LPGA Tour winner with two major titles, was born on this day in South Africa.

12.title: Steve Lowery born in Birmingham
12.text: Steve Lowery, a three-time PGA Tour winner, was born on this day in Birmingham, Alabama.

12.title: The PGA Championship is founded
12.text: The PGA of America staged the first PGA Championship in 1916, won by Jim Barnes, establishing one of golf's four major championships.

12.title: Cristie Kerr's path to World No. 1
12.text: Born in 1977, Cristie Kerr would go on to reach World No. 1 and win the 2007 U.S. Women's Open and 2010 LPGA Championship.

13.title: Arnold Palmer's final competitive round
13.text: Arnold Palmer played his final competitive round on this day, withdrawing after four holes of the Champions Tour's Administaff Small Business Classic, ending one of golf's greatest careers.

13.title: Tom Vardon dies in Minnesota
13.text: Tom Vardon, course architect and brother of Harry Vardon, died on this day in St. Paul, Minnesota, after a long career on both sides of the Atlantic.

13.title: Jimmy Walker wins Frys.com Open
13.text: Jimmy Walker won the Frys.com Open in California for his first PGA Tour title at age 34, the start of a breakout 2013-14 season.

13.title: Justin Thomas wins CJ Cup in Korea
13.text: Justin Thomas beat Danny Lee in a playoff to win the CJ Cup at Nine Bridges in South Korea for one of his multiple PGA Tour titles.

13.title: Hubert Green's major-winning era
13.text: Hubert Green, the 1977 U.S. Open and 1985 PGA Championship winner, was one of the steadiest American players of his generation with 19 PGA Tour titles.

14.title: Beth Daniel born in Charleston
14.text: Beth Daniel, a World Golf Hall of Fame member with 33 LPGA Tour wins and the 1990 LPGA Championship, was born on this day in Charleston, South Carolina.

14.title: J.C. Snead born in Virginia
14.text: J.C. Snead, nephew of Sam Snead and an eight-time PGA Tour winner, was born on this day in Hot Springs, Virginia.

14.title: Caddie Eddie Lowery born
14.text: Eddie Lowery, the 10-year-old caddie who famously carried Francis Ouimet's bag at the historic 1913 U.S. Open, was born on this day in Newton, Massachusetts.

14.title: Jonas Blixt wins Frys.com Open
14.text: Sweden's Jonas Blixt won the Frys.com Open at CordeValle in California for his first PGA Tour title.

14.title: Seve Ballesteros's autumn brilliance
14.text: Seve Ballesteros, who won the 1984 Open Championship at St Andrews, was at the height of his powers in the mid-1980s and a Ryder Cup hero for Europe.

15.title: Horton Smith born in Missouri
15.text: Horton Smith, winner of the first Masters in 1934 and again in 1936, was born on this day in Springfield, Missouri.

15.title: Horton Smith dies in Detroit
15.text: Horton Smith, the inaugural Masters champion, died on this day in Detroit at age 55, having been born and died on the same October date.

15.title: Len Mattiace born in New York
15.text: Len Mattiace, a two-time PGA Tour winner who lost a playoff to Mike Weir at the 2003 Masters, was born on this day in Mineola, New York.

15.title: Tiger Woods wins Disney Classic
15.text: Tiger Woods won the National Car Rental Golf Classic at Walt Disney World, part of one of the greatest single seasons in golf history with nine PGA Tour wins.

15.title: Horton Smith wins the first Masters
15.text: Horton Smith won the inaugural Masters Tournament in 1934 at Augusta National, the first of his two green jackets in the event's early years.

16.title: Old Tom Morris wins the Open at Prestwick
16.text: Old Tom Morris won the Open Championship at Prestwick in 1861, the first of his four Open titles in the championship's earliest years.

16.title: The professional game expands
16.text: The 1920s saw professional golf grow rapidly on both sides of the Atlantic, an era defined by figures like Walter Hagen and the young Bobby Jones.

16.title: Arnold Palmer's rise to stardom
16.text: By 1960 Arnold Palmer had become golf's most charismatic star, his go-for-broke style and growing army of fans transforming the sport's popularity.

16.title: Greg Norman's first major year
16.text: Greg Norman won the 1986 Open Championship and led all four majors after 54 holes that year, a 'Saturday Slam' that confirmed the Australian as the game's top player.

16.title: PGA Tour fall schedule era
16.text: The autumn of 2011 marked a transitional period for the PGA Tour's fall calendar, with events that would later form the wraparound and FedEx Cup Fall seasons.

17.title: The first Open Championship is held
17.text: The inaugural Open Championship was played at Prestwick in Scotland on October 17, 1860, with Willie Park Sr. winning over 36 holes to become golf's first champion.

17.title: Ernie Els born in Johannesburg
17.text: Ernie Els, the 'Big Easy' who won four major championships including two U.S. Opens and two Open Championships, was born on this day in Johannesburg, South Africa.

17.title: Willie Park Sr. becomes golf's first champion
17.text: Willie Park Sr. defeated Old Tom Morris by two strokes to win the very first Open Championship at Prestwick, launching the world's oldest major championship.

17.title: Vijay Singh's World No. 1 ascent
17.text: In the autumn of 2004 Vijay Singh ended Tiger Woods's long reign at World No. 1 during a remarkable season in which the Fijian won nine PGA Tour events.

17.title: Ernie Els's amateur emergence
17.text: Born in 1969, Ernie Els emerged from South Africa's strong amateur ranks to become one of the most successful international players of his generation.

18.title: Nick O'Hern born in Australia
18.text: Nick O'Hern, an Australian left-hander best known for twice defeating Tiger Woods in WGC Match Play and a two-time Australian Masters winner, was born on this day.

18.title: Fred Couples's banner season
18.text: Born in October 1959, Fred Couples reached the pinnacle of the game in 1992 with his Masters victory and a stretch atop the world rankings.

18.title: Jim Furyk's consistent autumn
18.text: Jim Furyk, the 2003 U.S. Open champion known for his unorthodox swing, remained a steady presence in PGA Tour events through the late 2000s.

18.title: Patrick Cantlay wins Zozo at Sherwood
18.text: Patrick Cantlay won the Zozo Championship, relocated to Sherwood Country Club in California due to the pandemic, holding off Jon Rahm and Justin Thomas.

18.title: Nick O'Hern's Match Play feats
18.text: Nick O'Hern, born in 1971, became one of the few players to beat Tiger Woods twice in WGC Match Play during Woods's dominant prime.

19.title: Louis Oosthuizen born in South Africa
19.text: Louis Oosthuizen, the 2010 Open Championship winner at St Andrews who has finished runner-up in all four majors, was born on this day in Mossel Bay, South Africa.

19.title: Ben Martin wins first Tour title at Shriners
19.text: Ben Martin won the Shriners Hospitals for Children Open at TPC Summerlin in Las Vegas for his first PGA Tour victory.

19.title: Louis Oosthuizen's breakthrough year
19.text: Born in 1982, Louis Oosthuizen broke through in 2010 with a runaway seven-stroke victory at the Open Championship on the Old Course at St Andrews.

19.title: A new generation of American stars
19.text: Following the 2008 U.S. Ryder Cup win at Valhalla, a wave of young Americans signaled a generational shift in the professional game.

19.title: South African golf's strong pipeline
19.text: South Africa continued to produce world-class talent into the 2000s, with Louis Oosthuizen among those who would carry forward the legacy of Player, Els, and Goosen.

20.title: Danielle Kang born
20.text: Danielle Kang, winner of the 2017 KPMG Women's PGA Championship and a multiple LPGA Tour winner, was born on this day in San Francisco, California.

20.title: Tiger Woods's record 2002 season
20.text: By late 2002 Tiger Woods had won both the Masters and U.S. Open, a dominant year that kept him atop the World Ranking and cemented his standing as the game's best.

20.title: Bryson DeChambeau's autumn surge
20.text: Bryson DeChambeau won multiple events in the autumn of 2018, a run that lifted the science-minded American into the world's top five.

20.title: Justin Thomas's Player of the Year run
20.text: Justin Thomas, the 2017 PGA Championship winner and FedEx Cup champion, capped his breakout season with PGA Tour Player of the Year honors.

20.title: Ian Baker-Finch's Open glory
20.text: Ian Baker-Finch, born October 24, 1960, won the 1991 Open Championship at Royal Birkdale with a brilliant front-nine charge in the final round.

21.title: Brooks Koepka wins CJ Cup, reaches World No. 1
21.text: Brooks Koepka won the CJ Cup at Nine Bridges in South Korea by four strokes, a victory that lifted him to World No. 1 for the first time, overtaking Dustin Johnson.

21.title: PGA Tour player Alex Smalley born
21.text: Alex Smalley, an American who reached the PGA Tour and contended in fall events, was born on this day.

21.title: Brooks Koepka's run to the top
21.text: With his 2018 CJ Cup win, Brooks Koepka added a worldwide title to a season that already included two major championships, confirming his place at the summit of the game.

21.title: Nick Faldo's two-major year
21.text: Nick Faldo won both the Masters and the Open Championship in 1990, a dominant season that confirmed the Englishman as the world's best player.

21.title: The global game expands in Asia
21.text: The autumn Asian swing grew steadily in the 2010s, with events in Korea, Japan, and China drawing many of the world's top players.

22.title: Tiger Woods caps a historic season
22.text: By late October 2000, Tiger Woods had completed one of golf's greatest seasons, winning three majors and nine PGA Tour events on his way to the 'Tiger Slam.'

22.title: Justin Thomas wins CJ Cup in Korea
22.text: Justin Thomas won the inaugural CJ Cup at Nine Bridges in South Korea, adding an international title to his breakout 2017 season.

22.title: Vijay Singh's nine-win season
22.text: In 2004 Vijay Singh won nine PGA Tour events and ended Tiger Woods's record run at World No. 1, one of the finest seasons of the modern era.

22.title: Hideki Matsuyama's worldwide tear
22.text: Hideki Matsuyama began a remarkable run in late 2016, winning multiple events across continents to climb into the world's top five.

22.title: PGA Tour autumn in Asia
22.text: The Zozo Championship in Japan and other autumn events drew top players as the PGA Tour's fall slate continued across Asia in 2023.

23.title: Chi-Chi Rodriguez born in Puerto Rico
23.text: Chi-Chi Rodriguez, a World Golf Hall of Fame member, eight-time PGA Tour winner, and beloved showman with 22 senior victories, was born on this day in Puerto Rico.

23.title: Harvey Penick born in Texas
23.text: Harvey Penick, the legendary teacher and author of the 'Little Red Book' who coached major champions at the University of Texas, was born on this day.

23.title: Justin Thomas wins CIMB Classic
23.text: Justin Thomas won the CIMB Classic at TPC Kuala Lumpur, finishing 23-under to beat Hideki Matsuyama by three strokes.

23.title: Chi-Chi Rodriguez's Hall of Fame honor
23.text: Chi-Chi Rodriguez was inducted into the World Golf Hall of Fame in 1992, recognition of a career that combined competitive success with showmanship and charity.

23.title: Harvey Penick's enduring influence
23.text: Harvey Penick's teachings, captured in his best-selling 'Little Red Book,' shaped generations of golfers including pupils Ben Crenshaw and Tom Kite.

24.title: Ian Baker-Finch born in Australia
24.text: Ian Baker-Finch, who won the 1991 Open Championship at Royal Birkdale, was born on this day in Australia. He later became a respected television commentator.

24.title: Hideki Matsuyama wins Zozo Championship
24.text: Hideki Matsuyama won the Zozo Championship in his native Japan, thrilling home fans the same year he became the first Japanese man to win the Masters.

24.title: LPGA pioneer Margie Masters born
24.text: Margie Masters, the first Australian woman to win on the LPGA Tour, was born on this day.

24.title: Ian Baker-Finch's Birkdale triumph
24.text: Ian Baker-Finch's 1991 Open Championship win featured a sparkling front-nine 29 in the final round at Royal Birkdale to pull clear of the field.

24.title: Matsuyama's landmark year
24.text: Hideki Matsuyama's 2021 season, capped by wins at home and his historic Masters victory, made him the most celebrated golfer in Japanese history.

25.title: Payne Stewart dies in plane crash
25.text: Payne Stewart, the reigning U.S. Open champion and three-time major winner, died in an airplane accident on this day at age 42, months after his dramatic winning putt at Pinehurst.

25.title: Xander Schauffele born in San Diego
25.text: Xander Schauffele, the 2024 PGA Championship and Open Championship winner and Olympic gold medalist, was born on this day in San Diego, California.

25.title: Denny Shute born
25.text: Denny Shute, winner of the 1933 Open Championship and back-to-back PGA Championships in 1936 and 1937, was born on this day.

25.title: Patrick Cantlay wins Zozo at Sherwood
25.text: Patrick Cantlay won the Zozo Championship at Sherwood Country Club in California, rallying from four shots back in the final round.

25.title: A nation mourns Payne Stewart
25.text: The golf world was stunned by the loss of Payne Stewart, remembered for his knickers-and-cap style and his emotional 1999 U.S. Open victory at Pinehurst.

26.title: Cesar Sanudo born in Mexico
26.text: Cesar Sanudo, the first Mexican to win on the PGA Tour with his 1970 Azalea Open victory, was born on this day.

26.title: Tiger Woods's first full pro autumn
26.text: In late 1997 Tiger Woods closed out his first full professional season as World No. 1 and PGA Tour Player of the Year following his historic Masters victory.

26.title: PGA Tour fall events take shape
26.text: The PGA Tour's autumn schedule gave rising players early-season chances at titles, a wraparound calendar that reshaped the path to the FedEx Cup.

26.title: Cesar Sanudo breaks new ground
26.text: Cesar Sanudo's 1970 Azalea Open win made him a trailblazer for Mexican golf and a forerunner of later stars from his country.

26.title: FedEx Cup Fall reaches its climax
26.text: The PGA Tour's FedEx Cup Fall events shaped the final standings late in the year, determining card status and exemptions for the next season.

27.title: Patty Sheehan born
27.text: Patty Sheehan, a World Golf Hall of Fame member with six major titles and 35 LPGA Tour wins, was born on this day.

27.title: Patty Sheehan's Hall of Fame career
27.text: Born in 1956, Patty Sheehan won majors across three decades and earned induction into the World Golf Hall of Fame in 1993.

27.title: Tiger Woods's dominant World No. 1 reign
27.text: By late 2002 Tiger Woods was in the midst of one of golf's longest runs atop the World Ranking, having won eight major titles before the age of 27.

27.title: Webb Simpson's steady career
27.text: Webb Simpson, the 2012 U.S. Open champion, remained a fixture on the PGA Tour and U.S. team rosters through the mid-2010s with consistent play.

27.title: Justin Thomas wins CJ Cup in Korea
27.text: Justin Thomas won the CJ Cup at Nine Bridges in South Korea in a playoff over Danny Lee, adding another autumn international title to his resume.

28.title: Tiger Woods wins Zozo for record-tying 82nd title
28.text: Tiger Woods won the inaugural Zozo Championship in Japan, completed on a typhoon-delayed Monday, to tie Sam Snead's all-time record of 82 PGA Tour victories.

28.title: Amateur great Gary Cowan born
28.text: Gary Cowan, a Canadian amateur who won the U.S. Amateur twice, in 1966 and 1971, was born on this day.

28.title: Xander Schauffele wins WGC-HSBC Champions
28.text: Xander Schauffele won the WGC-HSBC Champions in Shanghai, beating Tony Finau in a playoff for one of his early signature PGA Tour victories.

28.title: Tiger Woods matches Snead's win record
28.text: With his Zozo Championship victory, Tiger Woods equaled Sam Snead's record of 82 PGA Tour titles, a mark that had stood since 1965.

28.title: Tiger Woods holds all four majors
28.text: By late 2001 Tiger Woods had become the first player to hold all four professional major titles at once, the 'Tiger Slam,' cementing his dominance.

29.title: U.S. Amateur champion Nathaniel Crosby born
29.text: Nathaniel Crosby, son of entertainer Bing Crosby and the 1981 U.S. Amateur champion, was born on this day. He later captained the U.S. Walker Cup team.

29.title: Justin Rose wins WGC-HSBC Champions
29.text: Justin Rose erased an eight-stroke final-round deficit to beat Dustin Johnson by two and win the WGC-HSBC Champions in Shanghai, one of the great comebacks of the year.

29.title: Justin Rose's stunning Shanghai comeback
29.text: Justin Rose's WGC-HSBC Champions win came after a closing 67 erased a huge deficit, capping a strong stretch that included his 2016 Olympic gold medal.

29.title: Tiger Woods caps his greatest season
29.text: By the end of October 2000, Tiger Woods had completed a historic season of three majors and nine wins, a campaign widely regarded among the finest ever.

29.title: Nathaniel Crosby's amateur triumph
29.text: Nathaniel Crosby won the 1981 U.S. Amateur at The Olympic Club, prevailing on the 37th hole of the final near his family's San Francisco roots.

30.title: Hideki Matsuyama wins WGC-HSBC Champions
30.text: Hideki Matsuyama won the WGC-HSBC Champions in Shanghai by seven strokes, becoming the first Japanese golfer to win an individual World Golf Championship.

30.title: Matsuyama's worldwide breakthrough
30.text: Matsuyama's runaway WGC-HSBC win was one of four titles in a remarkable autumn stretch that established him among the world's elite players.

30.title: Tiger Woods's Player of the Year season
30.text: Tiger Woods finished the 2005 season as PGA Tour Player of the Year with six victories, including two majors, reasserting his place atop the world rankings.

30.title: Nick Faldo's dominant early-1990s run
30.text: Nick Faldo was the world's top-ranked player for much of the early 1990s, a run anchored by his 1990 and 1992 Open Championship victories.

30.title: Keegan Bradley's resurgent autumn
30.text: Keegan Bradley's strong autumn play in 2022 returned him toward U.S. team contention and the top of the FedEx Cup standings.

31.title: Lucas Herbert wins Butterfield Bermuda Championship
31.text: Australia's Lucas Herbert won the Butterfield Bermuda Championship by one stroke over Patrick Reed and Danny Lee for his first PGA Tour title.

31.title: Francesco Molinari wins WGC-HSBC Champions
31.text: Italy's Francesco Molinari won the WGC-HSBC Champions in Shanghai, holding off Lee Westwood for a landmark victory in the autumn Asian swing.

31.title: Molinari's rise for Italian golf
31.text: Francesco Molinari's WGC win helped establish him as Italy's finest player, a path that led to his 2018 Open Championship triumph and Ryder Cup heroics.

31.title: A somber close to October 1999
31.text: October 1999 ended in mourning across golf following the death of Payne Stewart, with players and fans honoring the popular champion.

31.title: Rory McIlroy's strong autumn form
31.text: Rory McIlroy closed a productive autumn in 2021, his recent CJ Cup victory in Las Vegas confirming a return to top form heading into the new year.
